original_abstract:
Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education is often presented as a universal remedy for economic instability, food insecurity, and environmental crises, as well as a primary engine for global influence. However, a closer look at regional and national agendas reveals deep disparities in how STEM policy is conceived and applied across the Global North and Global South. This study investigates the underlying drivers of STEM education within these two geo-economic blocks to better understand the contextual factors shaping modern practice. Given the urgent calls for international collaboration on the Sustainable Development Goals and pandemic response, analyzing these geopolitical tensions is more critical than ever.

Adopting a theoretical approach rooted in a literature review and informed by ideology critique, this study unmasks the salient themes driving the STEM enterprise. In the Global North, STEM education has historically been fueled by a neoliberal agenda, characterized by an ambition for political and military dominance, the mitigation of economic downturns, and a growing interest in extra-terrestrial colonization. Conversely, in the Global South, the focus remains on "massification with equity." These nations strive to redress colonial-era imbalances and expand access to education, yet they often face stagnation due to financial constraints and a persistent brain drain. 

The analysis reveals the rise of "convenient partnerships," where the Global North leverages the geographical and natural resources of the Global South for biomedical research and space exploration. We argue that for STEM collaborations to be truly transformative, they must move beyond these exploitative patterns toward a model of mutual benefit. This requires the Global North to actively redistribute the power it holds over STEM knowledge and policy. For the Global South, we recommend a "vocationalisation" of STEM that integrates the humanities, fostering a more balanced and humanistic approach to education that serves the needs of all citizens rather than just a neoliberal elite.
humanized_abstract:
STEM education is frequently described in global policy circles as the panacea for problems as diverse as economic crisis, food shortage and environmental protection as well as for increasing the global position of individual nations. Yet looking behind the regional and national science and technology policies and priorities of countries in the Global South and North, very different narratives and motivations concerning the promotion of STEM come to the surface. In this paper, the factors underlying these different motivations concerning the promotion of STEM are investigated. This is timely as there is a growing recognition of the need for co-operation internationally to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals, and to respond to the current pandemic.

Exploring the driving forces of the STEM enterprise by applying a critical literature review and an ideology critique, this paper identifies the focal points of the STEM project. While the STEM project in the Global North is a project that has a long history rooted in a neoliberal ideology to serve the interests of the ambition of global power and security in the age of the decline of the nation state, coping with economic crises and exploring the outer space? In the Global South, however, the focus is still “massification with equity” to address and redress the historical colonial imbalances in the education sector; to increase access and quality of education, but that is often hindered by unsustainable structural constraints such as financial constraints and brain drain.

Our analysis reveals the emergence of a new type of relationship that we call “convenient partnerships”. The Global North uses the geographical, climate, human and animal resources of the Global South for biomedical research and for space exploration. We do not think that current STEM collaborations will have the desired impact of improving the world if they do not evolve away from these rentier and exploitative relations, and establish more reciprocal relations. This means that the North must cede power in the world of STEM and the way it is governed. In the South, we need vocational training in STEM disciplines that include the humanities in order to provide all members of the population with a type of training that is humanistic and for a humanistic society that is no longer subject to the market fundamentalism of the neoliberal elites.
